slide_paragraph: This opening slide introduces the lecture topic: pre-training and scaling laws for large language models. The title signals that the lecture will move beyond using an existing LLM and discuss how large models are initially trained from raw text, how their size and training data affect performance, and why compute becomes a central limitation. The visual of a human head connected to digital components, a CPU, gears, documents, and a monitor suggests the connection between human-like language behavior and computational infrastructure. The key message is that modern generative AI is not only about model architecture, but also about the engineering decisions that make training possible: data filtering, model size, numerical precision, distributed GPUs, and compute-efficient scaling.
index_text: Slide 1 | Pre-training and scaling laws | introduction to LLM training, compute, scaling, and model development

slide_paragraph: This slide places pre-training inside the broader generative AI project lifecycle. The diagram is organized as a pipeline with four major phases: Scope, Select, Adapt and align model, and Application integration. In the Scope phase, the project begins by defining the use case, because the task determines what kind of model, data, and evaluation will be needed. In the Select phase, the practitioner chooses whether to use an existing model or pre-train a model from scratch. The Adapt and align phase includes prompt engineering, fine-tuning, alignment with human feedback, and evaluation, showing that using an LLM usually requires several refinement steps. Finally, Application integration covers optimizing and deploying the model for inference, then augmenting it to build LLM-powered applications. The slide emphasizes that pre-training is only one decision in a full system-building process.
index_text: Slide 2 | Generative AI project lifecycle | define use case, choose model, adapt, align, evaluate, deploy, integrate application

slide_title: Generative AI Project Lifecycle: Model Selection Focus
slide_paragraph: This slide repeats the generative AI project lifecycle to highlight the model-selection stage. The visual structure again shows a left-to-right workflow: define the use case, choose an existing model or pre-train your own, adapt and align the model, evaluate it, deploy it, and build applications around it. The important emphasis here is the transition from problem definition to model choice. A project may not always need a custom pre-trained model; often, an existing foundation model can be adapted through prompting, fine-tuning, or alignment. However, if the use case has special domain language, privacy constraints, uncommon modalities, or very high performance requirements, pre-training or continued pre-training may become relevant. The slide prepares the student to think of pre-training as a strategic choice, not a default step.
index_text: Slide 3 | Lifecycle focus | choosing existing model or pretraining your own before adaptation and deployment

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the main decision between using a pretrained foundation model and training a custom LLM. The left side refers to a pretrained LLM or foundation model, which is a general-purpose model already trained on large datasets and made available for reuse. The right side refers to training your own model, which leads to a custom LLM. The diagram suggests a spectrum: using an existing foundation model is usually faster and cheaper, while training a custom model gives more control but requires much more data, compute, and engineering. The main lesson is that model choice depends on constraints such as task specificity, available data, budget, performance needs, and whether the model must understand a specialized domain.
index_text: Slide 4 | Considerations for choosing a model | pretrained foundation model versus custom LLM trained from scratch

slide_title: Considerations for Choosing a Model: Foundation vs Custom
slide_paragraph: This slide reinforces the comparison between a foundation model and a custom LLM. A foundation model is already trained and can often be adapted with prompting, retrieval, fine-tuning, or alignment, which makes it practical for many projects. A custom LLM, by contrast, is created by training a model on selected data, giving the developer more control over vocabulary, domain coverage, architecture, and behavior. The visual arrangement shows that the custom path is connected to the phrase 'train your own model', while the foundation path is connected to 'pretrained LLM'. The student should understand that custom training is not simply better; it is more expensive and only justified when existing models cannot meet the requirements.
index_text: Slide 5 | Foundation model versus custom LLM | pretrained reuse compared with training your own model

slide_paragraph: This transition slide announces the next topic: model architectures and pre-training objectives. In LLMs, architecture describes the structural design of the model, such as encoder-only, decoder-only, or encoder-decoder Transformer designs. The pre-training objective describes the task used to teach the model from raw data before it is adapted to downstream use cases. For example, an encoder-only model may be trained to reconstruct masked words, while a decoder-only model may be trained to predict the next token. This distinction is important because the objective shapes what the model becomes good at: understanding, generation, or transforming one sequence into another.
index_text: Slide 6 | Model architectures and pre-training objectives | how architecture and training task shape LLM behavior

slide_title: LLM Pre-training at a High Level
slide_paragraph: This slide shows the high-level pre-training pipeline. On the left, massive unstructured text data is collected at GB, TB, or PB scale. Before the data reaches the model, it passes through a data quality filter, and the slide states that only about 1–3% of the original tokens may remain. This highlights that data cleaning is a major part of LLM training: low-quality, duplicated, unsafe, or irrelevant text must be removed. The filtered text is then used to train the LLM on GPUs. On the right, the vocabulary table shows token strings, token IDs, and embedding or vector representations. This means raw text is not fed to the model as characters directly; it is tokenized into units, mapped to numeric IDs, and represented as dense vectors that the neural network can process.
index_text: Slide 7 | LLM pre-training high level | unstructured data, quality filtering, token strings, token IDs, embeddings, vocabulary

slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes the three major Transformer families used in language modeling. Encoder-only models take an input and produce output representations, making them suitable for understanding tasks. Decoder-only models take an input sequence and generate output tokens autoregressively, making them common for generative LLMs. Encoder-decoder models include both components: the encoder reads and represents the input sequence, while the decoder generates a related output sequence. The diagram visually separates these designs into three columns, showing which components are present in each architecture. The key point is that all three are Transformer-based, but they are optimized for different pre-training objectives and downstream tasks.
index_text: Slide 8 | Transformers | encoder only, decoder only, and encoder decoder architectures

slide_paragraph: This slide explains encoder-only LLMs trained with masked language modeling. The original sentence shown is 'The teacher teaches the student.' In masked language modeling, some tokens are hidden or replaced with a mask, and the model learns to reconstruct the missing text. The slide labels the objective as reconstructing text, or denoising, because the model receives a corrupted version of the input and must recover the original. It also emphasizes bidirectional context: the encoder can look at both the words before and after a masked token. This is why autoencoding models are strong for understanding tasks, because each token representation can be informed by the full surrounding sentence.
index_text: Slide 9 | Autoencoding models | encoder-only LLM, MLM objective, reconstruct masked text, bidirectional context

slide_paragraph: This slide lists the typical use cases and examples of autoencoding models. Because these models learn rich bidirectional representations of text, they are well suited for tasks that require understanding rather than free-form generation. Sentiment analysis requires recognizing whether text expresses positive or negative opinion. Named entity recognition requires identifying spans such as people, organizations, and locations. Word classification assigns labels to words or tokens based on their context. The examples given are BERT and RoBERTa, both of which are encoder-based representation models. The slide teaches that if the task is mainly classification or extraction, an encoder-style model is often more appropriate than a purely generative decoder model.
index_text: Slide 10 | Autoencoding use cases | sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, word classification, BERT, RoBERTa

slide_paragraph: This slide explains decoder-only LLMs trained with causal language modeling. The sentence 'The teacher teaches the student' is used to show next-token prediction: given previous tokens such as 'The teacher', the model predicts the next token, such as 'teaches'. The objective is labeled as predicting the next token, and the context is labeled unidirectional because the model is only allowed to use earlier tokens, not future ones. This is essential for generation: when writing text left to right, the model cannot know future words in advance. The diagram on the right reinforces the decoder-only structure, where outputs are generated sequentially. This training objective is the foundation of many modern generative LLMs.
index_text: Slide 11 | Autoregressive models | CLM objective, decoder-only LLM, predict next token, unidirectional context

slide_paragraph: This slide lists the main strengths of autoregressive models. Their natural use case is text generation because they are trained to predict the next token repeatedly, forming complete sentences, paragraphs, answers, or code. The slide also mentions 'other emergent behavior', which refers to capabilities that appear as model size increases, such as following instructions, performing simple reasoning, translating, summarizing, or answering questions, even when those exact tasks were not explicitly programmed as separate modules. The examples given are GPT and BLOOM, both decoder-only style large language models. The key point is that generative behavior arises directly from the next-token prediction objective combined with scale and diverse training data.
index_text: Slide 12 | Autoregressive use cases | text generation, emergent behavior, GPT, BLOOM

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces encoder-decoder models trained with sequence-to-sequence objectives, specifically span corruption. The original text is again based on 'The teacher teaches the student.' Instead of masking a single word, a span of text is replaced with a sentinel token, shown as a special placeholder. The encoder reads the corrupted input, while the decoder reconstructs the missing span. This differs from standard masked language modeling because the model is not just labeling a hidden token in place; it is generating a sequence that fills the removed region. The slide therefore connects encoder-decoder architecture with tasks where an input sequence must be transformed into a new output sequence.
index_text: Slide 13 | Sequence-to-sequence models | encoder-decoder, span corruption, sentinel token, reconstruct missing span

slide_paragraph: This slide lists the tasks where encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence models are especially useful. Translation is a natural example because the input is a sentence in one language and the output is a sentence in another language. Text summarization also fits this architecture because the model reads a longer input document and generates a shorter output. Question answering can be formulated similarly: the input may include a question and context, and the output is the answer. The example models are T5 and BART, both of which are known for text-to-text framing. The key message is that encoder-decoder models are suitable when the task requires both strong input understanding and controlled output generation.
index_text: Slide 14 | Sequence-to-sequence use cases | translation, summarization, question answering, T5, BART

slide_title: Model Architectures and Pre-training Objectives Summary
slide_paragraph: This slide compares three architecture-objective combinations in one place. Autoencoding uses an encoder-only LLM and a masked language modeling objective: the input contains a mask, and the target is the original text. Decoder-only autoregressive modeling uses causal language modeling: given earlier tokens such as 'The teacher', the model predicts the next token, such as 'teaches'. Sequence-to-sequence modeling uses an encoder-decoder architecture with span corruption: a span is removed and replaced by a placeholder, and the model generates the missing span. The slide is important because it shows that architecture and training objective are linked. A model becomes useful for particular tasks because the pre-training objective teaches it a particular kind of language behavior.
index_text: Slide 15 | Architecture and pre-training objective summary | autoencoding MLM, autoregressive CLM, seq-to-seq span corruption

slide_title: The Significance of Scale: Task Ability
slide_paragraph: This slide contrasts BERT-base with 110 million parameters and BLOOM with 176 billion parameters to illustrate the significance of scale. The visual emphasis is that both are language models, but they exist at very different sizes. A larger parameter count generally means the model has more capacity to store patterns, represent linguistic structures, and support more complex behaviors. The phrase 'task ability' suggests that scale can change what a model is capable of doing, not merely improve a score slightly. The slide prepares the discussion of scaling laws by showing that model size is one of the central variables behind modern LLM capability.
index_text: Slide 16 | Significance of scale | BERT 110M versus BLOOM 176B and task capability

slide_paragraph: This slide shows the historical growth of model size over time. The timeline begins around 2018 with BERT-L at 340 million parameters, then moves to GPT-2 at 1.5 billion, GPT-3 at 175 billion, and PaLM at 540 billion by 2022. The listed drivers of this growth are the introduction of the Transformer, access to massive datasets, and more powerful compute resources. The slide teaches that modern LLM progress was not caused by a single improvement; it came from a combination of better architectures, much larger training corpora, and hardware capable of performing huge numbers of operations. This historical view motivates why scaling became a research topic.
index_text: Slide 17 | Model size over time | BERT-L 340M, GPT-2 1.5B, GPT-3 175B, PaLM 540B, growth drivers

slide_title: Model Size vs. Time: Toward Trillions
slide_paragraph: This slide visualizes model-size growth with increasingly large circles on a timeline. BERT-L appears as a small circle at 340 million parameters, GPT-2 grows to 1.5 billion, GPT-3 becomes much larger at 175 billion, and PaLM is shown as 540 billion. The arrow labeled 'increase?' points toward an even larger circle labeled 'Trillion(s)', suggesting the question of whether models should continue growing into trillion-parameter scale. The visual encourages students to think critically: if larger models often perform better, is the obvious strategy always to keep increasing parameter count? The later slides answer this by introducing compute budgets, memory constraints, and compute-optimal training.
index_text: Slide 18 | Model size versus time | increasingly large circles, possible trillion parameter models, question of continued scaling

slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces the computational challenges that arise when models become very large. Once models contain billions or hundreds of billions of parameters, training is no longer a simple matter of running a neural network on one GPU. Memory is needed to store model weights, gradients, optimizer states, and intermediate activations. Compute is needed for forward and backward passes across many tokens. Communication is needed when the training is distributed across multiple GPUs. The slide prepares students for the following quantitative discussion about GPU RAM, precision formats, quantization, and distributed training strategies.
index_text: Slide 19 | Computational challenges | memory, compute, GPU limits, large-scale LLM training

slide_title: Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters
slide_paragraph: This slide calculates the memory required just to store a one-billion-parameter model in 32-bit floating-point precision. A single FP32 parameter uses 4 bytes, so 1 billion parameters require approximately 4 × 10^9 bytes, which is about 4 GB of GPU RAM. The slide emphasizes that this is only storage for the model weights, not the full training memory. The calculation gives students an intuitive baseline: even a 1B-parameter model already consumes several gigabytes before gradients, optimizer states, activations, or batch data are considered. This simple multiplication is the foundation for understanding why large models quickly exceed the capacity of a single GPU.
index_text: Slide 20 | GPU RAM for 1B parameters | 1 parameter 4 bytes, 1B parameters about 4GB at FP32

slide_title: Additional GPU RAM Needed to Train 1B Parameters
slide_paragraph: This slide shows that training needs much more memory than inference or simple storage. It states that about 20 extra bytes per parameter may be needed during training. This extra memory comes from components such as optimizer states, gradients, and activations. For example, when using an optimizer like Adam, the training process stores additional moving-average statistics for each parameter. Gradients must also be stored after backpropagation, and activations may be kept to compute gradients. The main lesson is that a model that appears to fit in memory for inference may not fit for training, because training stores several additional tensors for every parameter.
index_text: Slide 21 | Additional GPU RAM for training | about 20 extra bytes per parameter beyond weights

slide_title: Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Train 1B Parameters
slide_paragraph: This slide combines the previous two memory estimates. A one-billion-parameter model requires about 4 GB to store the weights in 32-bit full precision, but approximately 24 GB to train in full precision after adding training-related memory. The difference is important: the memory needed to store a model is not the same as the memory needed to update it. Training requires the model weights plus gradients, optimizer states, and temporary activation memory. Therefore, when planning LLM training, one must estimate training memory, not just parameter storage. The slide provides a concrete rule of thumb for why even moderate-size models require powerful GPUs.
index_text: Slide 22 | GPU RAM to train 1B parameters | 4GB weights versus 24GB training memory at FP32

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces quantization as a way to represent model numbers using fewer bits. It compares FP32, which is 32-bit floating point, with lower-precision formats such as FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8. The number line highlights that different formats have different ranges and precision. FP32 can represent extremely large and small values, roughly from negative 3e38 to positive 3e38, while lower-precision formats reduce memory but may reduce numerical detail. The key idea is that neural network parameters are numbers, and choosing how many bits to store each number directly affects memory usage, speed, and sometimes accuracy. Quantization is therefore a practical tool for training and deploying large models.
index_text: Slide 23 | Quantization | FP32 versus FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8, range, memory, precision

slide_paragraph: This slide explains how FP32 stores a number using 32 bits. The bit layout is divided into 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction or mantissa bits. The sign bit tells whether the number is positive or negative, the exponent controls the scale or magnitude, and the fraction/mantissa stores the precision of the value. The example stores pi, 3.141592, and shows that the FP32 representation approximates it as 3.1415920257568359375, while the real value is 3.1415926535897932384. This illustrates an important principle: floating-point numbers are approximations. FP32 offers a wide range and relatively high precision, but it still cannot represent every real number exactly.
index_text: Slide 24 | Quantization FP32 | 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 fraction bits, storing pi approximately

slide_paragraph: This slide compares FP32 with FP16 using the example of storing pi. FP16 uses only 16 bits: 1 sign bit, 5 exponent bits, and 10 fraction bits. Because it has fewer bits than FP32, it needs only 2 bytes instead of 4 bytes per value. However, the reduction also limits the representable range and precision. The slide shows FP16 representing pi approximately as 3.140625, which is less precise than the FP32 approximation. It also shows the FP16 range from about -65504 to 65504, much smaller than FP32's extremely wide range. The key trade-off is memory savings versus numerical accuracy and range.
index_text: Slide 25 | Quantization FP16 | 2 byte half precision, 1 sign 5 exponent 10 fraction, pi rounded to 3.140625

slide_paragraph: This slide explains BFLOAT16, also called BF16, and compares it with FP32. BFLOAT16 uses 16 bits like FP16, so it also requires 2 bytes per value, but it keeps the same 8 exponent bits as FP32 and uses only 7 fraction bits. Because it preserves the exponent size, BFLOAT16 keeps a range close to FP32, roughly around plus or minus 3e38. However, because it has fewer fraction bits, it has lower precision. The slide describes BF16 as a truncated FP32, meaning it keeps the broad scale of FP32 while dropping many precision bits. This is why BFLOAT16 is popular in deep learning: it often provides stable training with lower memory usage.
index_text: Slide 26 | Quantization BFLOAT16 | 1 sign 8 exponent 7 fraction, FP32-like range with less precision

slide_paragraph: This slide presents INT8 quantization, where each value is stored using only 8 bits, requiring 1 byte of memory. The slide shows INT8 representing pi as the integer 3, demonstrating that integer formats are much less precise for raw real-valued quantities unless a scaling method is used. INT8 has a limited signed integer range from -128 to 127, unlike floating-point formats that represent fractional and very large values. The key intuition is that INT8 can greatly reduce memory and accelerate computation, especially during inference, but it requires careful scaling so that real-valued neural network weights and activations can be mapped into integer ranges without losing too much information.
index_text: Slide 27 | Quantization INT8 | 8-bit integer, range -128 to 127, stores pi as 3 without scaling

slide_title: Calculating Max and Min FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, INT8
slide_paragraph: This slide gives a detailed table for the maximum and minimum values representable by several numerical formats. For FP32, the table shows 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction bits, leading to a maximum around 3.4028235E+38 and a corresponding negative minimum. FP16 uses 5 exponent bits and 10 fraction bits, giving a much smaller maximum of 65504. BFLOAT16 uses 8 exponent bits and 7 fraction bits, giving a range close to FP32 but with lower precision. INT8 is different: it uses one sign-related bit and seven value bits in a signed integer representation, giving values from -128 to 127. The table connects bit allocation directly to numerical range.
index_text: Slide 28 | Max and min precision formats | FP32 FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8 bit layouts and representable ranges

slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes why quantization matters for LLMs. Quantization reduces the memory required to store and train models by projecting original 32-bit floating-point values into lower-precision spaces. The table compares FP32, FP16, BFLOAT16, and INT8 by bits, exponent size, fraction size, and memory per value. FP32 uses 4 bytes, FP16 and BFLOAT16 use 2 bytes, and INT8 uses 1 byte. The slide also mentions quantization-aware training, where the model learns scaling factors during training so it can operate better under quantized representations. BFLOAT16 is highlighted as a popular choice because it preserves a wide exponent range while saving memory.
index_text: Slide 29 | Quantization summary | lower precision memory savings, QAT, FP32 FP16 BFLOAT16 INT8 comparison

slide_title: Approximate GPU RAM Needed to Store 1B Parameters with Quantization
slide_paragraph: This slide revisits the 1B-parameter memory calculation under different precision choices. A full-precision FP32 model needs about 4 GB to store 1 billion parameters. If the model is stored in 16-bit precision, the memory drops to about 2 GB. If it is stored in 8-bit precision, the memory drops to about 1 GB. The visual clearly shows why quantization is so important for practical LLM deployment: reducing the number of bytes per parameter can make a model fit on cheaper or smaller hardware. The trade-off is that lower precision must be managed carefully to avoid unacceptable degradation in model quality.
index_text: Slide 30 | GPU RAM to store 1B parameters | 4GB FP32, 2GB 16-bit, 1GB 8-bit

slide_title: GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models
slide_paragraph: This slide scales the memory problem from one billion parameters to very large models. It shows that a 175B-parameter model may require around 4,200 GB of full-precision GPU RAM for training, while a 500B-parameter model may require around 12,000 GB. These numbers emphasize that training frontier-scale LLMs is far beyond the capacity of a single GPU. The memory includes more than weights; it also reflects training overhead such as gradients and optimizer states. The key lesson is that as model size grows, training becomes a distributed systems problem, not only a machine learning problem.
index_text: Slide 31 | GPU RAM for larger models | 175B needs about 4200GB, 500B needs about 12000GB at FP32 training

slide_title: GPU RAM Needed to Train Larger Models: Need for Multi-GPU Training
slide_paragraph: This slide makes the practical conclusion from the previous memory estimates: as model sizes get larger, the model must be split across multiple GPUs for training. The slide visually relates massive memory requirements to the need for distributed computation. A single GPU cannot hold all weights, gradients, optimizer states, and activations for a 175B or 500B parameter model in full precision. Therefore, engineers use strategies such as data parallelism, model parallelism, sharding, and memory offloading. The slide prepares students for distributed compute strategies by showing that they are not optional optimizations; they are necessary for training very large models.
index_text: Slide 32 | Need to split model across GPUs | larger models exceed single GPU memory

slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces efficient multi-GPU compute strategies. The purpose of these methods is to make training possible when the model, data, or training state cannot be handled efficiently on one GPU. Multi-GPU strategies must solve two problems at once: distributing work so training is faster, and distributing memory so the model fits. The following slides focus on approaches such as Distributed Data Parallel and Fully Sharded Data Parallel, which differ in what they replicate and what they shard. The key idea is that large LLM training depends heavily on systems-level design.
index_text: Slide 33 | Efficient multi-GPU compute strategies | distributed training methods for large LLMs

slide_paragraph: This slide distinguishes two reasons to use distributed compute. In one case, the model is too big for a single GPU, so the model itself or its training state must be split across devices. In another case, the model fits on one GPU, but training data can be processed in parallel to speed up training. The diagram shows two LLM blocks, indicating that distributed compute can be about memory capacity or about throughput. This is an important distinction: data parallelism primarily accelerates training by using multiple data batches, while sharding and model parallel methods address memory limitations. Choosing the right strategy depends on whether the bottleneck is model size, data volume, or training speed.
index_text: Slide 34 | When to use distributed compute | model too big for one GPU or data parallel training

slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates Distributed Data Parallel training. A dataloader sends different mini-batches to several GPUs, labeled GPU 0 through GPU 3. Each GPU holds a full copy of the LLM and independently performs a forward and backward pass on its assigned data. After backpropagation, the gradients from all GPUs are synchronized so every model replica receives the same averaged update. Then each GPU updates its local copy of the model. The main advantage is speed: several batches are processed at the same time. The limitation is memory: because every GPU stores a full model copy plus training states, DDP does not solve the problem of a model being too large to fit on one GPU.
index_text: Slide 35 | Distributed Data Parallel | dataloader splits batches, each GPU runs forward backward, gradients synchronized, model updated

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces Fully Sharded Data Parallel, motivated by the ZeRO paper. The phrase 'zero data overlap between GPUs' means that instead of every GPU storing a complete copy of training states, parts of the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states can be distributed across GPUs. FSDP is designed to reduce memory redundancy, allowing larger models to be trained with the same hardware. The slide references ZeRO and PyTorch FSDP work, connecting the practical technique to research on memory optimization for trillion-parameter training. The main idea is that sharding reduces per-GPU memory by making each GPU responsible for only part of the full training state.
index_text: Slide 36 | Fully Sharded Data Parallel | ZeRO motivation, zero data overlap, sharding model states across GPUs

slide_title: Recap: Additional GPU RAM Needed for Training
slide_paragraph: This slide gives a memory breakdown per parameter during training. The table shows model parameters or weights requiring 4 bytes per parameter in FP32. Adam optimizer states require about 8 bytes per parameter because Adam keeps two state tensors. Gradients add another 4 bytes per parameter. Activations and temporary memory may add around 8 bytes per parameter as a high-end estimate. The total is summarized as 4 bytes for the parameter itself plus about 20 extra bytes per parameter for training overhead. This table explains why training memory is much larger than inference memory: training keeps multiple related tensors for each learnable weight.
index_text: Slide 37 | Training memory recap | weights 4 bytes, Adam states 8, gradients 4, activations 8, total about 24 bytes

slide_paragraph: This slide shows the memory behavior of Distributed Data Parallel. The key bullet states that there is one full copy of the model and training parameters on each GPU. The diagram uses colored memory blocks for parameters, gradients, and optimizer states, and shows that the same full stack is repeated across GPU 0 through GPU N−1. This repetition is why DDP is simple and effective for speed, but inefficient for memory. If a model cannot fit on one GPU, DDP alone will not fix that, because each GPU still needs the complete model and training state. The student should understand DDP as replication-based parallelism.
index_text: Slide 38 | Memory usage in DDP | full copy of parameters gradients optimizer states on each GPU

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces ZeRO as a strategy to reduce memory by distributing, or sharding, the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs. The diagram contrasts a baseline where each GPU stores repeated memory blocks with sharded layouts where each GPU stores only a subset. A model shard is defined as a subset of parameters assigned to each GPU. The key intuition is that redundancy wastes memory: if four GPUs all store the same optimizer states, the total cluster memory is not being used efficiently. ZeRO reduces this redundancy so larger models can be trained without requiring every GPU to hold the full training state.
index_text: Slide 39 | ZeRO optimizer | sharding parameters gradients and optimizer states across GPUs to reduce memory redundancy

slide_paragraph: This slide expands ZeRO into Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3. The diagram shows progressively more aggressive sharding across GPUs. Stage 1 shards optimizer states, reducing the memory used by optimizer statistics. Stage 2 also shards gradients, further reducing duplicated memory after backpropagation. Stage 3 shards parameters as well, giving the largest memory savings but requiring more communication because GPUs must gather needed weights during computation. The main lesson is that memory efficiency increases across stages, but communication and implementation complexity also increase. ZeRO provides a structured way to trade communication cost for memory savings.
index_text: Slide 40 | ZeRO stages | Stage 1 optimizer states, Stage 2 gradients, Stage 3 parameters sharded across GPUs

slide_paragraph: This slide again shows the DDP workflow, emphasizing the order of operations. The dataloader distributes data to GPU 0, GPU 1, GPU 2, and GPU 3. Each GPU runs a forward and backward pass using its local full model copy. Then gradients are synchronized across devices. After synchronization, each GPU updates its own model copy, keeping all replicas identical. This repeated diagram helps students remember that DDP is data-parallel rather than memory-sharded: the data changes from GPU to GPU, but the model is replicated. It is excellent when the model fits on each GPU and the goal is faster training over more data.
index_text: Slide 41 | DDP workflow | dataloader, GPU replicas, forward backward pass, gradient sync, update model

slide_title: Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) with GPU Memory Focus
slide_paragraph: This slide presents DDP again but with the LLM icons removed from the GPU boxes, making the workflow more abstract. The same sequence is visible: data is distributed, each GPU performs forward and backward computation, gradients are synchronized, and models are updated. The abstraction helps focus on the parallel process rather than model internals. The main concept remains that DDP parallelizes over data: every GPU computes gradients on a different mini-batch, then all gradients are combined so the final update is equivalent to training with a larger batch. This strategy improves throughput but keeps memory duplication.
index_text: Slide 42 | DDP abstract workflow | GPUs process batches in parallel, synchronize gradients, update replicated model

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the FSDP workflow using the same broad layout as DDP: dataloader, GPUs, forward/backward passes, gradient synchronization, and model updates. The key difference, implied by the FSDP title, is that the model and training states are not fully replicated on each GPU. Instead, they are sharded, so each GPU stores only a portion of the full state. During computation, the necessary pieces are gathered when needed, then released or re-sharded afterward. This enables training larger models than DDP under the same memory budget. The slide prepares for the more detailed FSDP diagrams that show weight gathering explicitly.
index_text: Slide 43 | FSDP workflow | similar training loop to DDP but with model states sharded across GPUs

slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) Training Steps
slide_paragraph: This slide continues the FSDP workflow and emphasizes that forward and backward computation still occurs on each GPU, followed by gradient synchronization and model updates. The difference from DDP is not the existence of forward/backward passes, but how model weights and training states are stored and retrieved. In FSDP, GPUs may need to communicate to assemble the required weights for a layer before computation, then shard them again to save memory. The visual reinforces that FSDP is still a form of data parallelism, but with full sharding added to reduce per-device memory.
index_text: Slide 44 | FSDP training steps | forward and backward passes with sharded model states and synchronization

slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Getting Weights
slide_paragraph: This slide shows a more detailed FSDP process. On the left, each GPU stores only small colored shards rather than the full model state. Before the forward pass, the system performs a 'Get weights' operation, meaning the GPUs gather the necessary parameter shards to compute the next part of the model. After the forward pass, another get-weights stage appears before the backward pass, because gradients require the relevant weights and activations. Finally, gradients are synchronized and the model is updated. The pink arrows highlight the communication points. The key idea is that FSDP saves memory by storing shards, but it pays extra communication cost to temporarily assemble weights when needed.
index_text: Slide 45 | FSDP get weights | parameter shards gathered for forward and backward passes, then gradients synchronized

slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Synchronizing Gradients
slide_paragraph: This slide highlights the gradient synchronization stage in FSDP. After weights are gathered and each GPU completes its forward and backward passes, gradients must be coordinated so that the sharded model updates remain consistent. The highlighted 'Synchronize gradients' block shows that even though memory is sharded, training still requires communication between GPUs. The diagram then leads to update-model blocks for each GPU. The important lesson is that FSDP reduces memory usage but does not eliminate synchronization; it changes when and what GPUs communicate. Students should see FSDP as a balance between memory savings and communication overhead.
index_text: Slide 46 | FSDP synchronization | gradients synchronized after forward backward with sharded weights

slide_title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP): Sharding Options
slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes practical FSDP features. It states that FSDP helps reduce overall GPU memory utilization and supports offloading to CPU if needed. CPU offloading means some states can be stored in CPU memory instead of GPU memory, trading speed for capacity. The slide also mentions configuring the level of sharding via a sharding factor. The bottom visual compares full replication, full sharding, and hybrid sharding. Full replication resembles DDP and uses more memory. Full sharding maximizes memory savings. Hybrid sharding sits between them, grouping GPUs so some replication and some sharding occur. The best choice depends on model size, GPU count, and communication bandwidth.
index_text: Slide 47 | FSDP sharding options | full replication, full sharding, hybrid sharding, CPU offloading

slide_paragraph: This transition slide introduces scaling laws and compute-optimal models. Scaling laws describe empirical relationships between model performance and factors such as model size, dataset size, and training compute. Compute-optimal modeling asks how to spend a fixed compute budget most effectively: should we train a larger model, train on more data, or balance both? This topic is central because bigger models are expensive, and simply increasing parameter count may not be the best use of resources. The next slides examine how performance changes with scale and why recent work argues for balancing parameters and training tokens.
index_text: Slide 48 | Scaling laws and compute optimal models | performance versus model size data size and compute

slide_paragraph: This slide frames pre-training as an optimization problem. The goal is to maximize model performance, or equivalently minimize loss. The constraint is the compute budget, including GPUs, training time, and financial cost. Within that budget, there are two major scaling choices: dataset size, measured as the number of tokens used for training, and model size, measured as the number of parameters. The diagram shows these three quantities as a connected system: compute budget supports the choices of data and model size, and those choices affect performance. The key lesson is that good pre-training is not just 'make the model bigger'; it is choosing a balanced combination of data, parameters, and compute.
index_text: Slide 49 | Scaling choices | maximize performance under compute budget by choosing tokens and parameters

slide_paragraph: This slide defines the unit 'petaflop/s-day'. A petaflop per second is one quadrillion floating-point operations per second, written as 1,000,000,000,000,000 operations per second. A petaflop/s-day means sustaining that compute rate for one full day. The slide connects this unit to NVIDIA V100 GPUs and notes that one petaflop/s-day corresponds to those chips running at full efficiency for 24 hours. The purpose of the unit is to give a common way to compare the training cost of different LLMs. Instead of saying only how many GPUs were used, PF-days combine compute rate and time into a single measure.
index_text: Slide 50 | Compute budget | petaflop per second day definition, one quadrillion operations per second for a day

slide_title: Compute Budget for Training LLMs: GPU Examples
slide_paragraph: This slide visualizes petaflop/s-days using rows of NVIDIA V100 and A100 GPUs. The diagram shows that different GPU types can supply the same total compute with different numbers of chips because their individual performance differs. The bracketed groups indicate that a certain collection of GPUs running at full efficiency for 24 hours is equivalent to one petaflop/s-day. The main idea is that compute budget combines hardware capability and training duration. Faster GPUs or more GPUs can produce the same compute in less time, while fewer or slower GPUs require longer training.
index_text: Slide 51 | Petaflop/s-day GPU examples | V100 and A100 groups running for 24 hours

slide_title: Number of Petaflop/s-Days to Pre-train Various LLMs
slide_paragraph: This slide shows a bar chart comparing the training compute required for several LLM families, including BERT/RoBERTa, T5, and GPT-3. The y-axis is training petaflop/s-days on a logarithmic scale, meaning each step represents a large multiplicative increase. Smaller BERT and RoBERTa models require much less compute than larger T5 and GPT-3 models. Within GPT-3, the bars grow from small to medium to large to XL and then to billions of parameters, with the 175B model requiring the most compute. The chart makes clear that pre-training cost rises dramatically with model size, which motivates compute-efficient scaling and careful budget allocation.
index_text: Slide 52 | PF-days for pretraining LLMs | bar chart comparing BERT RoBERTa T5 GPT-3 compute requirements

slide_paragraph: This slide connects compute budget to model performance using test loss. The left diagram shows compute budget balancing dataset size and model size. The right plot places test loss on the vertical axis and compute on the horizontal axis. The downward trend indicates that as compute increases, test loss tends to decrease, meaning performance improves. The line is shown on a log-style scale and comes from Kaplan et al.'s scaling laws. The key intuition is that more compute allows either more data, a larger model, longer training, or some combination of these. However, the improvement follows a smooth trend with diminishing returns, so each additional compute increase gives smaller relative gains.
index_text: Slide 53 | Compute budget versus model performance | test loss decreases as compute increases, dataset and model size balance

slide_title: Dataset Size and Model Size vs. Performance
slide_paragraph: This slide emphasizes that compute resources are constrained by hardware, project timeline, and financial budget. The visual triangle places compute budget, dataset size, and model size as interacting variables. The snowflake symbols suggest that some variables may be fixed or limited in an experiment. If compute is fixed, increasing model size may require reducing training tokens, and increasing dataset size may require using a smaller model or training fewer steps. The key message is that scaling choices are trade-offs. A practical team must decide how to allocate limited compute between parameter count and data quantity rather than assuming both can grow freely.
index_text: Slide 54 | Dataset size and model size versus performance | compute constraints from hardware timeline and budget

slide_title: Dataset Size and Model Size vs. Performance: Scaling Trends
slide_paragraph: This slide shows two plots: test loss decreases as dataset size increases, and test loss also decreases as the number of parameters increases. The frozen compute-budget icon suggests that these relationships are studied under constraints. The highlighted labels for dataset size and model size show that both are important levers. The downward lines indicate that more data and more parameters can both improve performance, but the improvement is not unlimited; it follows a power-law-like trend with diminishing returns. The central lesson is that performance depends on scaling both the data and the model, and focusing on only one can become inefficient.
index_text: Slide 55 | Dataset and model scaling trends | test loss decreases with more tokens and more parameters under compute constraints

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Chinchilla paper by Hoffmann and colleagues, titled 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models.' The visible abstract text highlights the central result: many large language models were significantly under-trained, meaning they had many parameters but were not trained on enough tokens for their compute budget. The highlighted Chinchilla result compares a 70B-parameter model trained on much more data with larger models such as Gopher. The key claim is that for a fixed compute budget, model size and number of training tokens should be scaled together. The slide marks an important shift from simply building larger models toward building compute-optimal models.
index_text: Slide 56 | Chinchilla paper | Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models, smaller model with more tokens can outperform larger under-trained models

slide_paragraph: This slide explains the core idea of compute-optimal training. Under a fixed compute budget, one can choose a very large model with relatively fewer training tokens, but this may produce an over-parameterized and under-trained model. Alternatively, one can choose a smaller model and train it on more data. The bullet points state that very large models may be over-parameterized and under-trained, and that smaller models trained on more data could perform as well as large models. The diagram contrasts model size and dataset size under the same compute budget. The lesson is that the best model is not necessarily the largest; it is the model whose parameter count and training tokens are balanced for the available compute.
index_text: Slide 57 | Compute optimal models | very large models can be undertrained, smaller models with more data can match performance

slide_title: Chinchilla Scaling Laws for Model and Dataset Size
slide_paragraph: This slide presents the Chinchilla scaling rule that compute-optimal training data size is roughly 20 times the number of parameters. The table compares Chinchilla, LLaMA-65B, GPT-3, OPT-175B, and BLOOM. Chinchilla has 70B parameters and about 1.4T actual tokens, matching the compute-optimal estimate. LLaMA-65B has 65B parameters and about 1.4T actual tokens, also close to the estimate. By contrast, GPT-3 and similar 175B models would require around 3.5T tokens under the 20× rule, but their actual token counts are much smaller, such as 300B or 180B. The red box highlights under-training relative to the Chinchilla recommendation.
index_text: Slide 58 | Chinchilla scaling laws | compute optimal tokens about 20x parameters, Chinchilla and LLaMA close, GPT-3 OPT BLOOM undertrained

slide_title: Model Size vs. Time: Decrease After Scaling Debate
slide_paragraph: This slide revisits the model-size timeline and adds later models such as LLaMA 65B and BloombergGPT 50B. Earlier slides asked whether model sizes would keep increasing toward trillions. Here, the added smaller circles suggest a possible decrease or correction after the Chinchilla insight. Instead of always increasing parameters, researchers began training smaller models with more tokens and better data. BloombergGPT 50B is highlighted, and LLaMA 65B is shown near it, indicating that competitive models can be smaller than PaLM 540B or GPT-3 175B if trained more efficiently. The slide emphasizes a shift from parameter maximalism to compute-optimal design.
index_text: Slide 59 | Model size versus time updated | after PaLM, LLaMA 65B and BloombergGPT 50B suggest smaller compute-optimized models

slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes the three main factors that determine LLM performance: model size N, dataset size D, and compute C measured in PF-days. Model size refers to the number of parameters excluding embeddings, dataset size refers to the amount of pretraining data, and compute refers to the operations used for training. The slide defines a petaflop/s-day as performing 10^15 neural network operations per second for one day, roughly 10^20 operations per day. It also notes that an A100 GPU is around 312 TFLOPs, approximately 3e14 FLOPs, or about 2.7e19 FLOPs per day. The visible formula suggests a parameter-count approximation involving model width and number of layers, reinforcing that architecture dimensions determine model size.
index_text: Slide 60 | Performance of LLMs | model size, data size, compute, PF-day definition, A100 FLOPs, tokens trained on

slide_paragraph: This slide breaks down how Transformer parameters scale by component. The self-attention Q, K, and V projections each use d_model by d_model matrices, giving three matrices and roughly 3 × d_model squared parameters. The attention output projection adds another d_model squared matrix. The feed-forward network has two large linear layers: one expands from d_model to about 4d_model, and the other projects back, contributing roughly 8 × d_model squared parameters together. Layer normalization and biases are marked negligible compared with these large matrices. The table shows why total parameter count scales mainly with d_model squared and the number of layers: wider models become much larger very quickly.
index_text: Slide 61 | LLM architecture | parameter matrix shapes for QKV, output projection, feedforward layers, scaling as d_model squared

slide_paragraph: This slide compares GPT-3 from 2020 and LLaMA from 2023. GPT-3 is shown with a vocabulary size of 50,257, context length 2048, 175B parameters, and training on 300B tokens. LLaMA is shown with a vocabulary size of 32,000, context length 2048, 65B parameters, and training on about 1–1.4T tokens. The training-cost notes estimate GPT-3 as requiring thousands to ten-thousands of V100 GPUs, about one month of training, and millions of dollars. For LLaMA 65B, the slide lists 2,048 A100 GPUs, 21 days of training, and about $5M. The comparison highlights that LLaMA used fewer parameters but many more tokens, reflecting the compute-optimal trend.
index_text: Slide 62 | Two example models | GPT-3 175B 300B tokens versus LLaMA 65B 1-1.4T tokens and training resources

slide_title: One GPU for One Day: Scaling Trade-off Thought Experiment
slide_paragraph: This slide presents a practical thought experiment: if you can use one GPU for one day, should you train a 5 million parameter language model on 100 books, a 500 million parameter model on one book, or a 100k parameter model on 5k books? The question forces students to think about balancing model size and dataset size under a fixed compute budget. A very large model with too little data may overfit or be under-trained. A tiny model with lots of data may lack capacity. A middle option may be more balanced, depending on the task and compute. The slide prepares the intuition behind scaling laws: performance is determined by a relationship between parameters, tokens, and compute, not by any single factor alone.
index_text: Slide 63 | One GPU one day | compare small model many books versus large model little data under fixed budget

slide_title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models: Kaplan et al., 2020
slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Kaplan et al. 2020 paper 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models.' The slide shows the paper title and authors, including Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Brown, Alec Radford, Dario Amodei, and others. The paper is central to the lecture because it empirically studied how language model loss changes as model size, dataset size, and compute are scaled. It proposed smooth power-law relationships that made it possible to predict performance trends from scale. The slide functions as a reference point: the next slides summarize the findings and then later compare them with the Chinchilla/Hoffmann view.
index_text: Slide 64 | Kaplan et al. 2020 | Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models paper and authors

slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Three Power-Law Trends
slide_paragraph: This slide shows three plots from the Kaplan scaling-law analysis. The first plot relates test loss to compute measured in PF-days, showing that more compute reduces loss. The second plot relates loss to dataset size in tokens, showing that more training data also reduces loss. The third plot relates loss to non-embedding parameters, showing that larger models reduce loss. The figure caption states that language modeling performance improves smoothly as model size, dataset size, and compute increase, and that all three must be scaled together for optimal performance. The key concept is the power-law relationship: improvements are predictable and smooth, but they also exhibit diminishing returns.
index_text: Slide 65 | Summary of scaling laws | plots of test loss versus compute, dataset size, and parameters

slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Equations and Critical Values
slide_paragraph: This slide presents a table of Kaplan-style scaling relationships. For model size, the loss is written as a power-law function of N with exponent 0.076 and a critical value around 8.8 × 10^13 parameters; a 10× increase gives about 17% lower loss. For dataset size, the exponent is 0.095 with a critical value around 5.4 × 10^13 tokens; a 10× increase gives about 20% lower loss. For compute, the exponent is 0.050 with a critical value around 3.1 × 10^8 PF-days; a 10× increase gives about 11% lower loss. The interpretation column emphasizes diminishing returns and the need to balance model and data.
index_text: Slide 66 | Scaling laws table | L(N), L(D), L(Cmin), exponents 0.076 0.095 0.050, critical values and loss improvement

slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Formal Statements
slide_paragraph: This slide shows formal mathematical statements from the scaling laws paper. It describes how, for models trained with a limited number of parameters and sufficiently large datasets, loss follows a power-law with model size. It also describes how, for large models trained with limited datasets, loss follows a power-law with dataset size. Finally, it states that when training is compute-limited and model size and dataset size are chosen optimally, loss follows a power-law with compute. The equations are important because they turn empirical observations into predictive relationships. The student should understand that these are not exact physical laws; they are fitted empirical laws that describe trends over a range of experiments.
index_text: Slide 67 | Formal scaling laws | equations for loss as function of model size data size and compute

slide_title: Summary of Scaling Laws: Interpretation Table
slide_paragraph: This slide repeats the scaling-law table in a clearer summary form. It organizes the findings by scaling type: model size, dataset size, and compute. Each row includes the equation, critical value, approximate loss improvement for a 10× increase, and interpretation. The model-size row says larger models lower loss but with diminishing returns. The dataset-size row says more training data lowers loss, also with diminishing returns. The compute row says more compute lowers loss only if model and data are balanced. The table is useful because it directly links mathematical formulas to practical training decisions: where should extra resources be spent? The answer depends on whether the current model is parameter-limited, data-limited, or compute-limited.
index_text: Slide 68 | Scaling law summary table | model data compute rows with equations critical values and practical interpretations

slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes key observations from scaling-law research. Performance depends strongly on scale: model parameters, data size, and compute used for training. It depends more weakly on model shape, such as exact depth or width, within the studied range. The slide states that performance versus scale can be modeled with power laws, meaning that predictable curves describe how loss changes as resources increase. It also emphasizes that performance improves most when model size and dataset size are scaled together; increasing one while keeping the other fixed leads to diminishing returns. Finally, larger models are described as more sample efficient because they can reach the same loss with fewer steps or data points than smaller models.
index_text: Slide 69 | Scaling observations | performance depends on scale, weakly on shape, scale model and data together, larger models sample efficient

slide_paragraph: This slide focuses on how model size affects training loss. The plots show training curves for models of different sizes, with larger models improving faster and reaching lower loss than smaller models when enough compute and data are available. A highlighted note states that larger models require fewer samples to reach the same performance, which is the idea of sample efficiency. Another note indicates that the optimal model size grows smoothly with the loss target and compute budget. The key message is that increasing model size is powerful, but only when the model receives enough training data and compute to use that capacity effectively.
index_text: Slide 70 | Model size effect | larger models need fewer samples and can reach lower loss when trained enough

slide_paragraph: This slide provides additional Kaplan-style comparisons. The left plot is labeled loss versus model and dataset size, showing how validation loss changes when both data and parameters vary. The right plot compares loss versus model size and training steps, with curves that show how training duration interacts with model size. The figure caption emphasizes that early stopping a larger model can be more compute-efficient than fully training a smaller model in some settings. The slide reinforces that model performance cannot be understood from parameter count alone. Training steps, dataset size, and compute allocation all affect the final loss.
index_text: Slide 71 | Additional comparisons | loss versus model and dataset size, loss versus model size and training steps

slide_paragraph: This slide explains compute per sample using the equation: compute per sample equals batch size multiplied by number of steps multiplied by parameters, divided by dataset size. The example underneath substitutes batch size 100, number of steps 10, and a parameter count written as a power of ten, divided by dataset size. The plot above shows validation loss across different compute budgets and training settings, illustrating that larger compute enables better performance when allocated well. The equation helps students see that compute is not only hardware; it depends on training configuration. Larger batches, more steps, and more parameters all increase compute, while dataset size changes how that compute is distributed per sample.
index_text: Slide 72 | Compute effect | compute per sample equation batch size times steps times parameters divided by dataset size

slide_paragraph: This slide presents a table estimating parameter counts and forward-pass FLOPs per token for Transformer components. The rows include embedding, attention QKV projections, attention mask computation, attention output projection, feedforward layers, and de-embedding. For example, QKV attention parameters scale with the number of layers, model dimension, and attention dimension, while feedforward parameters scale with model dimension and feedforward width. The total non-embedding parameter formula is shown as N = 2 d_model n_layer (2 d_attn + d_ff). The forward compute is summarized as C_forward = 2N + 2 n_layer n_ctx d_attn. The table omits smaller terms such as biases, nonlinearities, and layer normalization, focusing on dominant costs.
index_text: Slide 73 | FLOPs per token | table of parameter counts and compute for embeddings attention feedforward and de-embedding

slide_paragraph: This slide defines the symbols used in the FLOPs-per-token calculation. n_vocab is the vocabulary size, meaning the number of token types the model can represent. n_ctx is the context length or sequence length. d_model is the model width, also called embedding or hidden size. d_attn is the attention projection size, often approximately equal to d_model. d_ff is the feedforward or MLP inner width, often about four times d_model. n_layer is the number of Transformer layers or blocks. These definitions are essential because compute and parameter formulas become meaningful only when each variable is understood.
index_text: Slide 74 | FLOPs variable definitions | vocabulary size, context length, model width, attention size, feedforward width, layers

slide_title: Why 2 FLOPs per Parameter in Forward Pass?
slide_paragraph: This slide explains the common rule of thumb that each parameter contributes roughly 2 FLOPs in a forward pass. FLOP 1 is multiplication: a unit i multiplies its activation h(i) by a weight w to send a contribution to unit j. FLOP 2 is addition: unit j adds that contribution into its total input a(j). The diagram shows connected units where activations flow through weighted connections. In a linear layer, this multiply-add pattern happens for many weights, so the compute cost is approximately twice the number of parameters involved. This simplification is useful for estimating LLM training cost without counting every minor operation.
index_text: Slide 75 | Why 2 FLOPs per parameter | one multiply and one add for each weight contribution in forward pass

slide_paragraph: This slide critiques the Kaplan scaling-law results. It states that Kaplan et al. used the same learning-rate schedule for all training runs regardless of how many training tokens or batches were used. The slide explains that the learning-rate schedule should be adjusted based on the number of training steps; otherwise, it can impair performance. If some models are not trained with suitable schedules, the measured loss may not reflect the true best performance for that compute budget. Therefore, the resulting scaling laws may be flawed or biased. This sets up the motivation for later work, especially the Hoffmann/Chinchilla paper, which revised conclusions about how to allocate compute.
index_text: Slide 76 | Issues with Kaplan laws | same learning rate schedule for all runs may flaw scaling conclusions

slide_title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models: Hoffmann et al., 2022
slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the Hoffmann et al. 2022 paper 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,' also associated with the Chinchilla result. The slide shows the paper title and authors, marking it as the alternative or correction to the earlier Kaplan conclusions. The key contribution of this paper is that compute-optimal training requires increasing the amount of training data much more than earlier practice suggested. Instead of using a very large model trained on relatively fewer tokens, the paper argues that smaller models trained on many more tokens can achieve better performance under the same compute budget. This slide prepares students for the Kaplan versus Hoffmann comparison.
index_text: Slide 77 | Hoffmann et al 2022 | Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models, Chinchilla scaling revision

slide_paragraph: This slide directly compares the two scaling recommendations. Kaplan et al. 2020 suggested that when compute increases, one should prioritize increasing model size more than data size. The slide states that with a 10× compute increase, Kaplan would increase model size by 5× and data size by 2×; with a 100× compute increase, model size by 25× and data by 4×. Hoffmann et al. 2022 argued instead that model size and data size should increase at the same rate. With a 10× compute increase, both should grow by about 3.1×; with a 100× compute increase, both should grow by 10×. The key difference is parameter-heavy scaling versus balanced parameter-token scaling.
index_text: Slide 78 | Kaplan versus Hoffmann | Kaplan prioritizes model size, Hoffmann scales model and data at same rate

slide_paragraph: This slide shows a table comparing models by parameter count and training tokens. It lists LaMDA at 137B parameters and 168B tokens, GPT-3 at 175B and 300B tokens, Jurassic at 178B and 300B tokens, Gopher at 280B and 300B tokens, MT-NLG at 530B and 270B tokens, and Chinchilla at 70B and 1.4T tokens. The contrast is striking: Chinchilla is much smaller than many listed models but uses far more training tokens. The slide illustrates the practical implication of compute-optimal training: many earlier large models had high parameter counts but comparatively low token counts, while Chinchilla reallocates compute toward more data.
index_text: Slide 79 | Allocate compute budget | table comparing model parameters and training tokens, Chinchilla 70B with 1.4T tokens

slide_title: How to Allocate Compute Budget: Tokens per Parameter
slide_paragraph: This slide gives the final rule of thumb from Chinchilla: to train a compute-optimal model, use about 20 training tokens for every parameter. The table shows ratios for Chinchilla, LLaMA, Phi-3, and Llama 3, illustrating that modern models often use hundreds or thousands of tokens per parameter, especially smaller but heavily trained models. The TLDR at the bottom says Chinchilla recommends around 20 tokens per parameter, but modern models may use approximately 20 times or more tokens per parameter. The key lesson is that data quantity has become central: effective LLM design is no longer only about choosing a large parameter count, but about matching parameters with enough tokens.
index_text: Slide 80 | Compute budget allocation | Chinchilla says about 20 tokens per parameter, modern models often use more

slide_paragraph: This closing slide lists the main references used for the lecture. The first is the DeepLearning.AI course 'Generative AI with Large Language Models,' which provides broad educational coverage of LLM development. The second is Kaplan et al. 2020, 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models,' which introduced influential empirical scaling relationships for loss, data, model size, and compute. The third is Hoffmann et al. 2022, 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,' which revised compute allocation recommendations through the Chinchilla result. The fourth is a lecture on scaling laws of LLMs by Prof. Pawan Goyal from NPTEL IIT Kharagpur. These references show that the lecture combines course material, research papers, and academic lectures.
index_text: Slide 81 | References | DeepLearning.AI Generative AI with LLMs, Kaplan scaling laws, Hoffmann Chinchilla, NPTEL lecture
